content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 25, 2017

Diane Elliott
4621 Crestwood Way
Sacramento, Ca 95822

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-17-185

Dear Ms. Elliott:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance.²

Please note that we provide advice under the post-government employment provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, Section 1090, or Public Contract Code Section 10411. Additionally, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTIONS

1. As the retired Capital Outlay Program Manager with the Department of General Services ("DGS"), are you prohibited under the "permanent ban" from participating on behalf of your employer, Kitchell CEM ("Kitchell"), in a request for qualifications ("RFQ") reissued by the Department of State Hospitals ("DSH") for an Infrastructure Master Plan for some or all of the Department's five state hospitals?

2. Under the Act, are you precluded from participating in other RFQ solicitations and projects advertised by DSH for architecture/engineering or construction project management services?

3. For purposes of the one-year ban, would you be eligible to participate in projects administered by DGS starting on April 28, 2018?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 84113; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-17-185
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Generally, the permanent ban applies where you have participated in a project as a public official by taking part in proceedings personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential information. However, the permanent ban would not bar your participation on behalf of your employer in an RFQ reissued by DSH for Master Planning and Condition Assessment services if (1) the RFQ does not constitute a continuation of earlier proceedings, (2) you did not participate in the proceedings, or (3) your participation in the proceedings was merely in an oversight role.

In addition, the one-year ban would prohibit you from appearing or communicating before your former agency on behalf of your employer for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act such as the awarding of an RFP or contract for services prior to April 28, 2018. Therefore, you may not be identified in any RFQ prior to that time.

2. As a Kitchell employee, you would be precluded from participating in any future RFQ solicitations and projects advertised by DSH for architecture/engineering or construction project management services if you participated in any of the projects as a public official by taking part in proceedings personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential information.

Additionally, you may not appear before or communicate with your former agency on behalf of your employer for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act such as the awarding of an RFP or contract for services for one-year after leaving state service.

3. Yes. You would be able to appear before your former agency on April 28, 2018 for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act such as the awarding of an RFP or contract for services because that appearance would be more than one year after you ceased performing your duties as a Capital Outlay Program Manager. However, as noted above, you may not be identified (or listed as a Senior Project Manager) in any RFQ prior to the expiration of the one-year period.

FACTS

You are currently employed as a Senior Project Manager by Kitchell in Sacramento. Kitchell assists customers with all phases of the construction process including project and construction management, architecture, engineering and other construction industry-related services.

On April 27, 2017, you ceased performing your duties as a Capital Outlay Program Manager with DGS. You held this position for six years from 2011 to 2017. You used accrued leave credits until May 31, 2017, when you separated from State Service. You subsequently retired on June 1, 2017.

File No. I-17-185
Page No. 3

Prior to this management position, you served as a Project Director III (supervisory) for 13
years and a Project Director II (professional) for 7 years. During your tenure at DGS, you were
required to file a Form 700 annually.

Kitchell plans to pursue a future RFQ with DSH for architecture, engineering, or
construction project management services. Prior to June 2015, DSH utilized DGS to procure and
manage these types of services.

From approximately October 2014 to June 2017, your duties as a Capital Outlay Program
Manager included oversight of all DSH projects managed by DGS. Projects included major capital
outlay, infrastructure repair, and special repair projects at all five DSH campuses.

DGS was responsible for solicitations of architectural, engineering, and construction project
management firms, fee negotiations, professional contracts, bidding of construction, awards and
contracts to general construction contractors, and management of projects through the construction
phase and turn-over to DSH.

Although you were involved with DSH projects while employed at DGS, you did not work
for DSH or the California Health and Human Services Agency.

To your knowledge, DSH has advertised only once for an RFQ project that included a
master plan study and facility condition assessment encompassing the five DSH campuses
statewide. This RFQ was advertised in late 2015. The proposed scope of work for the project,
estimated fees, contracting, and management of the selected professional consultants was
administered entirely by DSH.

Your former employer, DGS, had no role in the development or funding of the project or in
the professional selection process, procurement, or management of the contract. You personally had
no role in this project. Your current employer, Kitchell, submitted an RFQ for this project but was
not selected. DSH proceeded with the project solely under its own management, but due to a
number of circumstances, the project was put on hold, and the executed contract with the
professional firm was cancelled.

DSH intends to advertise for a subsequent solicitation for a similar project and set of
services in the coming months. There is no specific RFQ advertised yet. If this project is re-
advertised and your current employer submits qualifications, you will likely be listed as a Senior
Project Manager in the proposal.

Your employer, Kitchell, pursues Requests for Qualifications and contracts with DGS for
architecture, engineering and construction project management services on a regular basis. Many of
these solicitations are for client agencies of DGS who lack the authority to contract for these types
of professional services or choose to use DGS to contract for these services on their behalf.

FileNo. 1-17-185
Page No. 4

ANALYSIS

Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-governmental employment provisions under the Act: the one-year ban and the permanent ban.

These provisions are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions. (Sections 87400-87406 and Regulation 18746.1.)

One-Year Ban

The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any formal or informal appearance or any oral or written communication, before his or her former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1.)

You ceased performing your duties as a Capital Outlay Program Manager with DGS on April 27, 2017. Therefore, you are subject to the one-year ban until April 28, 2018, and you may not make a formal or informal appearance before DGS on behalf of your employer or a client for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act, such as the awarding of an RFP or contract for services.

Because the one-year ban would prohibit you from appearing or communicating before DGS on behalf of your employer prior to April 28, 2018, you may not be listed as a Senior Project Manager (or named in any other manner) in any RFQ prior to that time.

However, you may provide advice to your employer, Kitchell, regarding a proposed RFP or other contract, if you are not identified with these efforts and so long as you are not otherwise prohibited from such representation under the permanent ban.

Permanent Ban

The "permanent ban" prohibits the representation for compensation of any person, other than the State of California, in any proceeding you participated in as a public official. Section 87401 states:

"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his or her employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person (other than the State of California) before any court or state administrative agency or any officer or employee thereof by making any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication with the intent to influence, in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding if both of the following apply:

"(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial interest.

File No. 1-17-185
Page No. 5

"(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state administrative official
participated. Section 87402 applies this ban to assisting others in such
representation: No former state administrative official, after the termination of his
or her employment or term of office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel,
consult or assist in representing any other person (except the State of California)
in any proceeding in which the official would be prohibited from appearing under
Section 87401."

As the former Capital Outlay Program Manager with DGS, you are subject to the permanent
ban stated in Sections 87401 and 87402.

A "judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" is "any proceeding, application, request for a
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest
or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative
agency . . .." (Section 87400(c) and Regulation 18741.1(a)(3).) It does not include proceedings that
do not involve specific parties, such as the making of policies or rules of general application.
(Black Advice Letter, No. A-98-320.)

Previous Participation

An official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the
proceeding personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of confidential
information as an officer or employee . . .." (Section 87400(d).)

A supervisor is deemed to have participated personally and substantially in any proceeding
that was pending before the agency under his or her direct supervisory authority. A proceeding is
under a supervisor's "authority" if the supervisor's duties include direct involvement as follows:

(A) The supervisor's duties include the primary responsibility within the agency
for directing the operation or function of the program where the proceeding is
initiated or conducted. However, this provision does not apply to a supervisor
who is only responsible for the general oversight of the administrative actions or
functions of a program in which the responsibilities concerning the specific or
final review of the proceeding are expressly delegated to other persons in the
agency.

(B) The supervisor directly supervises the person performing the investigation,
review, or other action involved in the proceeding including, but not limited to,
assigning the matter for which the required conduct is taken.

(C) The supervisor reviews, discusses or authorizes any action in the
proceeding.

(D) The supervisor has contact with any of the participants in the proceeding
regarding the subject of the proceeding. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(4).)

File No. I-17-185
Page No. 6

Mere administrative oversight of programs, without reviewing or otherwise directly
supervising matters involving specific individuals, projects, or decisions under those programs,
does not rise to the level of personal and substantial involvement implicated by the statute. (In re
Lucas (2000) 14 FPPC Ops. 14.)

To the extent that your interaction with DSH's RFQ proceedings was "mere administrative
oversight of programs, without reviewing or otherwise directly supervising matters involving
specific individuals, projects, or decisions under those programs" it does not rise to the level of
"personal and substantial involvement" in the proceedings. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(4)(A) and In re
Lucas, supra.)

Therefore, the permanent ban would not bar your participation on behalf of your employer
in an RFQ issued by DSH for Master Planning and Condition Assessment services if the RFQ does
not constitute a continuation of earlier proceedings, you did not participate in the proceedings, or
your participation in the proceedings was merely in an oversight role.

Additionally, as a Kitchell employee, you would only be precluded from participating in any
future RFQ solicitations and projects advertised by DSH for architecture, engineering or
construction project management services if you participated in any of the projects as a public
official by taking part in proceedings personally, and substantially through decision, approval,
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation,
or use of confidential information.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By: Emelyn C. Rodriguez
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ECR:jgl
